subUnits.title: 2.1 Meaning, Processes and Regions of Volcanicity
notes: Volcanicity:
• The process by which molten rock (magma), gases, and ash are expelled from Earth's interior to the surface
• Results in the formation of volcanic landforms and features
• Driven by heat from Earth's interior and plate tectonic processes
Volcanic Processes:
1. Magma Generation:
• Magma forms when rock melts due to:
- Increased temperature (e.g., mantle plumes)
- Decreased pressure (decompression melting at divergent boundaries)
- Addition of volatiles (water lowers melting point at subduction zones)
• Magma composition varies: basaltic (low silica, fluid), andesitic, rhyolitic (high silica, viscous)
2. Magma Movement:
• Magma rises because it is less dense than surrounding rock
• Moves through fractures, weaknesses, or creates new pathways
• May accumulate in magma chambers before eruption
3. Eruption Types:
• Effusive (Gentle): Low-viscosity basaltic magma flows easily
- Example: Hawaiian volcanoes (Kilauea)
- Features: Lava flows, shield volcanoes
• Explosive (Violent): High-viscosity magma traps gases → pressure buildup → explosion
- Example: Mount St. Helens (1980), Mount Pinatubo (1991)
- Features: Ash clouds, pyroclastic flows, stratovolcanoes
4. Emission of Materials:
• Lava: Molten rock that reaches the surface
• Pyroclastics: Fragmented material ejected during explosive eruptions
- Ash (<2 mm), Lapilli (2-64 mm), Bombs/blocks (>64 mm)
• Volcanic gases: Water vapor (most abundant), CO₂, SO₂, HCl
- Can affect climate (SO₂ → sulfate aerosols → cooling)
Intrusive vs Extrusive Features:
INTRUSIVE (Plutonic) FEATURES: Form when magma cools below surface
1. Batholiths:
• Massive, irregular bodies of intrusive rock
• Exposed after erosion removes overlying rock
• Example: Jos Plateau granite (Nigeria)
2. Dykes:
• Sheet-like intrusions cutting across existing rock layers
• Often vertical or steeply inclined
3. Sills:
• Sheet-like intrusions parallel to existing rock layers
• Often horizontal or gently inclined
4. Laccoliths:
• Dome-shaped intrusions that lift overlying strata
• Example: Henry Mountains (Utah, USA)
5. Volcanic Plugs/Necks:
• Solidified magma in volcanic vent, exposed after erosion
• Example: Zuma Rock (Nigeria) – though debated if volcanic plug
EXTRUSIVE (Volcanic) FEATURES: Form when magma reaches surface
1. Volcanic Cones:
• Cinder/scoria cones: Built from pyroclastic fragments, steep slopes
• Composite/stratovolcanoes: Alternating layers of lava and ash, steep (e.g., Fuji, Vesuvius)
• Shield volcanoes: Broad, gentle slopes from fluid lava (e.g., Mauna Loa)
2. Lava Plateaus:
• Extensive, flat areas built by successive fluid lava flows
• Example: Deccan Plateau (India), Columbia Plateau (USA)
3. Calderas:
• Large, basin-shaped depressions formed by:
a) Collapse after massive eruption empties magma chamber
b) Explosive eruption removing mountain top
• Example: Crater Lake (Oregon, USA)
4. Geysers and Hot Springs:
• Heated groundwater emerges at surface
• Geysers: Intermittent eruptions of hot water/steam
• Hot springs: Continuous flow of warm water
Major Volcanic Regions of the World:
1. Circum-Pacific Belt (Ring of Fire):
• ~75% of world's active volcanoes
• Subduction zones around Pacific Ocean
• Countries: Japan, Indonesia, Philippines, Chile, USA (West Coast)
2. Mediterranean-Asiatic Belt:
• ~15% of world's volcanoes
• Collision zones: Mediterranean, Middle East, Himalayas
• Countries: Italy (Vesuvius, Etna), Greece, Turkey, Iran
3. Mid-Ocean Ridges:
• Divergent boundaries with underwater volcanism
• Example: Mid-Atlantic Ridge (Iceland sits on it)
4. Hotspot Volcanism:
• Fixed mantle plumes create volcanoes as plates move over them
• Example: Hawaiian Islands, Yellowstone (USA)
Nigerian Context:
• Nigeria has limited active volcanism but notable volcanic features:
- Jos Plateau: Ancient volcanic activity, granite intrusions
- Biu Plateau (Borno): Basaltic lava flows, volcanic plugs
- Mount Cameroon (border with Cameroon): Only active volcano in West Africa
• Volcanic soils in some areas support agriculture (e.g., Jos Plateau)

quiz.q: Volcanicity refers to:
opts: The expulsion of magma, gases, and ash from Earth's interior
quiz.explanation: Volcanicity encompasses all processes by which molten rock and volcanic materials reach Earth's surface.

quiz.q: Magma forms when rock melts due to all EXCEPT:
quiz.explanation: Rock melts due to increased temperature, decreased pressure (decompression), or addition of volatiles like water; decreased temperature causes solidification.

quiz.q: Effusive eruptions are characterized by:
opts: Gentle lava flows of low-viscosity magma
quiz.explanation: Effusive eruptions involve fluid basaltic magma that flows easily, forming shield volcanoes and lava plateaus.

quiz.q: An intrusive volcanic feature is:
quiz.explanation: Batholiths form when magma cools and solidifies below the surface; lava flows, cones, and ash are extrusive features.

quiz.q: A dyke is an intrusive feature that:
quiz.explanation: Dykes are sheet-like intrusions that cut discordantly across pre-existing rock strata.

quiz.q: Shield volcanoes are formed by:
opts: Gentle eruptions of fluid basaltic lava
quiz.explanation: Shield volcanoes have broad, gentle slopes built by successive flows of low-viscosity basaltic lava.

quiz.q: The Pacific Ring of Fire contains approximately what percentage of the world's active volcanoes?
quiz.explanation: About 75% of Earth's active volcanoes are located along the Circum-Pacific Belt due to extensive subduction zones.

quiz.q: A caldera is formed primarily by:
opts: Collapse after a massive eruption empties the magma chamber
quiz.explanation: Calderas are large depressions formed when a volcano's magma chamber empties during a massive eruption, causing the overlying land to collapse.

quiz.q: Mount Cameroon is significant in West Africa because it is:
opts: The only active volcano in the region
quiz.explanation: Mount Cameroon is West Africa's only active volcano, with recent eruptions (e.g., 2000, 2012) demonstrating ongoing volcanic activity.

quiz.q: Volcanic soils are often fertile because they:
opts: Contain abundant minerals from weathered volcanic rock
quiz.explanation: Volcanic soils are rich in minerals like potassium, phosphorus, and trace elements released from weathered volcanic materials, supporting agriculture.

subUnits.title: 2.2 Characteristic Landforms of Volcanic Regions
notes: Characteristic Landforms in Volcanic Regions:
INTRUSIVE LANDFORMS (Exposed by Erosion):
1. Batholiths and Stocks:
• Batholiths: Massive, irregular intrusive bodies (>100 km²)
- Form deep crustal magma chambers
- Exposed after millions of years of erosion
- Example: Jos Plateau granite (Nigeria), Sierra Nevada Batholith (USA)
• Stocks: Smaller intrusive bodies (<100 km²)
• Characteristics:
- Coarse-grained texture (slow cooling underground)
- Often form rugged, resistant uplands when exposed
- Source of minerals (tin, gold, copper in Nigeria's Jos Plateau)
2. Dykes and Sills:
• Dykes: Vertical or steeply inclined sheet intrusions cutting across strata
- Often form ridges or walls when erosion removes softer surrounding rock
- Example: Giant's Causeway (Northern Ireland) – columnar basalt dykes
• Sills: Horizontal or gently inclined sheet intrusions parallel to strata
- Form plateaus or escarpments when exposed
- Example: Great Whin Sill (England)
3. Laccoliths:
• Dome-shaped intrusions that arch overlying strata upward
• Form isolated hills or mountains when exposed
• Example: Henry Mountains (Utah, USA)
4. Volcanic Plugs/Necks:
• Solidified magma that filled a volcanic vent
• Resistant to erosion, stand as isolated peaks after surrounding cone erodes
• Examples: Zuma Rock (Nigeria, debated), Devil's Tower (Wyoming, USA)
EXTRUSIVE LANDFORMS (Formed at Surface):
1. Volcanic Cones:
a) Cinder/Scoria Cones:
- Built from pyroclastic fragments (ash, cinders, bombs)
- Steep slopes (30-40°), small size (<500 m high)
- Often occur on flanks of larger volcanoes
- Example: Parícutin (Mexico, grew in a cornfield 1943-1952)
b) Composite/Stratovolcanoes:
- Alternating layers of lava flows and pyroclastic deposits
- Steep, symmetrical cones (e.g., Fuji, Vesuvius, Kilimanjaro)
- Associated with explosive eruptions at subduction zones
- Hazards: Pyroclastic flows, lahars (mudflows), ash fall
c) Shield Volcanoes:
- Broad, gently sloping domes built by fluid basaltic lava
- Largest volcanoes on Earth (e.g., Mauna Loa: 4,169 m above sea level, 9,170 m from base)
- Effusive eruptions with lava flows, not explosive
2. Lava Plateaus and Plains:
• Extensive, flat to gently rolling areas built by successive fluid lava flows
• Individual flows may be 10-100 m thick, covering thousands of km²
• Examples:
- Deccan Plateau (India): ~500,000 km² of basalt
- Columbia Plateau (USA): ~164,000 km²
- Biu Plateau (Nigeria): Basaltic lava flows in Borno State
3. Calderas:
• Large, circular to oval depressions (1-100 km diameter)
• Formation mechanisms:
a) Collapse caldera: Magma chamber empties → roof collapses
b) Explosive caldera: Massive eruption removes mountain summit
• Often fill with water to form crater lakes
• Examples: Crater Lake (Oregon), Lake Toba (Indonesia), Ngorongoro Crater (Tanzania)
4. Geothermal Features:
a) Geysers:
- Intermittent eruptions of hot water and steam
- Require: heat source, water supply, constricted plumbing
- Example: Old Faithful (Yellowstone, USA)
b) Hot Springs:
- Continuous flow of heated groundwater to surface
- Used for bathing, heating, geothermal energy
- Example: Wikki Warm Springs (Bauchi, Nigeria)
c) Fumaroles:
- Openings emitting steam and volcanic gases
- Common in active volcanic areas
5. Volcanic Islands:
• Formed by submarine volcanoes that build above sea level
• Types:
- Oceanic islands: Hotspot or subduction-related (Hawaii, Japan)
- Continental fragments: Volcanism on continental crust (Iceland)
Economic and Environmental Importance of Volcanic Regions:
1. Economic Benefits:
• Mineral resources: Tin, gold, copper, diamonds in volcanic rocks
• Fertile soils: Volcanic ash enriches soil for agriculture
• Geothermal energy: Harnessing heat for electricity (Iceland, Kenya)
• Tourism: Volcanic landscapes attract visitors (Mount Fuji, Crater Lake)
• Construction materials: Basalt, pumice, volcanic ash for cement
2. Environmental Roles:
• Climate regulation: Volcanic aerosols can cool climate temporarily
• Land creation: Volcanic islands and plateaus add to Earth's surface
• Biodiversity: Unique ecosystems on volcanic soils and islands
3. Hazards and Risks:
• Direct: Lava flows, pyroclastic flows, ash fall, volcanic gases
• Indirect: Lahars (volcanic mudflows), tsunamis, climate impacts
• Long-term: Soil degradation, water contamination
Volcanic Hazards Management:
• Monitoring: Seismometers, gas sensors, ground deformation measurements
• Hazard mapping: Identifying areas at risk from different volcanic hazards
• Early warning systems: Evacuation plans based on eruption forecasts
• Land-use planning: Restricting development in high-risk zones
• Public education: Teaching communities about volcanic risks and responses
Nigerian Examples:
• Jos Plateau: Ancient volcanic activity, granite batholiths, tin mining
• Biu Plateau: Basaltic lava flows, volcanic plugs, fertile soils for agriculture
• Mount Cameroon: Active stratovolcano, fertile volcanic soils, tourism, hazards
• Wikki Warm Springs: Geothermal feature in Bauchi State, tourism potential

quiz.q: A batholith is best described as:
opts: A massive intrusive igneous body exposed by erosion
quiz.explanation: Batholiths are large, deep-seated intrusive igneous bodies that become exposed at the surface after extensive erosion of overlying rock.

quiz.q: Dykes differ from sills because dykes:
quiz.explanation: Dykes are discordant intrusions that cut across pre-existing rock strata, while sills are concordant and parallel to strata.

quiz.q: Shield volcanoes are characterized by:
opts: Broad, gentle slopes from fluid lava flows
quiz.explanation: Shield volcanoes have broad, gently sloping profiles built by successive flows of low-viscosity basaltic lava.

quiz.q: A caldera is formed primarily by:
opts: Collapse after a massive eruption empties the magma chamber
quiz.explanation: Calderas form when a volcano's magma chamber is emptied during a massive eruption, causing the overlying land to collapse into a large depression.

quiz.q: Geysers require all of the following EXCEPT:
quiz.explanation: Geysers require heat, water, and a constricted plumbing system to build pressure; atmospheric pressure variations can trigger eruptions but aren't a requirement.

quiz.q: The Jos Plateau in Nigeria is significant for volcanicity because it:
opts: Contains ancient granite batholiths and mineral resources
quiz.explanation: The Jos Plateau features ancient intrusive igneous rocks (granite batholiths) from past volcanic activity, associated with tin and other mineral deposits.

quiz.q: Volcanic soils are agriculturally valuable because they:
opts: Contain abundant minerals from weathered volcanic material
quiz.explanation: Volcanic soils are rich in essential plant nutrients (potassium, phosphorus, trace elements) released from weathered volcanic rocks.

quiz.q: Pyroclastic flows are dangerous because they:
opts: Are fast-moving, hot clouds of gas and volcanic debris
quiz.explanation: Pyroclastic flows are extremely hazardous, fast-moving (up to 700 km/h), superheated (up to 1,000°C) mixtures of gas and volcanic particles.

quiz.q: Mount Cameroon is classified as a:
quiz.explanation: Mount Cameroon is a stratovolcano (composite volcano) with steep slopes built from alternating layers of lava and pyroclastic material.

quiz.q: Geothermal energy from volcanic regions is harnessed by:
opts: Using heat from underground to generate electricity
quiz.explanation: Geothermal power plants use steam or hot water from volcanic areas to drive turbines and generate electricity, providing renewable energy.
